content: Idea Validation Framework — YC Startup School: The single most important question before building: 'Do people want this enough to pay for it?' The validation stack: Step 1 — Talk to 20 potential users before writing one line of code. Step 2 — Ask about their problem, not your solution. Step 3 — Look for pain, not politeness. People are polite about ideas they would never pay for. Step 4 — Pre-sell before building. If you cannot get 5 people to pay in advance, the market signal is weak. Paul Graham: 'It's better to make a few people really happy than to make a lot of people semi-happy.' Start narrow. Depth before breadth.

MVP in a Weekend — Build Fast Framework: The Weekend MVP rules. Day 1 morning: Define the ONE core action your product enables. Everything else is a future feature. Day 1 afternoon: Build the simplest version using no-code tools (Webflow, Glide, Notion, Tally). Day 2 morning: Put it in front of 5 real people. Watch where they get confused. Day 2 afternoon: Fix the three biggest friction points. Ship. No-code MVP stack for founders: Landing page — Webflow or Framer. Forms — Tally. Database — Airtable. Automation — Make or Zapier. Payments — Stripe. Time to first paying customer: 48-72 hours is the target. Your goal is a signal, not a product.

Fundraising Fundamentals — From Zero to Seed: The fundraising reality check: Most startups should not raise VC money. Ask first — can this business be profitable at small scale? If yes, consider bootstrapping. If network effects or capital intensity require scale, fundraising makes sense. The funding ladder: Friends and Family ($10K-$100K) → Pre-seed Angel ($100K-$500K) → Seed ($500K-$3M) → Series A ($3M-$15M). What investors actually buy: team, market size, traction signal, and the founder's ability to learn fast. Your deck needs six things: Problem, Solution, Market Size, Traction, Team, Ask. The founder story is as important as the business story. Why you? Why now? Why this?

Finding Your First 100 Customers: The distribution problem kills more startups than the product problem. Customer acquisition channels ranked by founder-friendliness: 1. Direct outreach (LinkedIn, email, WhatsApp) — highest conversion, most effort. 2. Community presence (Reddit, Discord, Slack groups) — medium conversion, builds trust. 3. Content marketing (Twitter/X, YouTube, newsletters) — slow to start, compounds. 4. Paid ads — fast feedback on messaging, burns cash without product-market fit. The rule: do things that don't scale first. Hand-deliver value to your first 100 users. Know their names. Understand their lives. The insights from manual effort become the engine of scale.

B2B SaaS Sales Fundamentals: B2B SaaS sales is a human skill before it is a technical one. The sales process: Discovery (understand their pain deeply) → Demo (show the pain solved, not features) → Proposal (simple, clear, ROI-focused) → Close (ask directly). The three objections and how to handle them: 'Too expensive' — anchor to cost of the problem, not the product price. 'Not the right time' — 'When would be the right time? What would need to change?' 'Need to check with the team' — 'Can I join that conversation?' Pricing principle: charge more than you think you should. Founders consistently underprice. Price signals quality. Start with annual contracts.
